築山古墳は、多くの大小古墳に囲まれており、築山駅前すぐ南のインキ山古墳（前方後円墳）、[[大谷山自然公園]]の中に残る複数の古墳、かん山古墳（築山古墳の北にある築山児童公園内：帆立貝式前方後円墳）、古屋敷古墳（築山古墳の西北：石室露出）、[[茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|茶臼山古墳]]（築山古墳の南西：直径50mの円墳）、前述の狐井塚古墳（築山古墳の南東）、コンピラ山古墳（築山古墳の東）等がある。 関係性があると思われるが、[[築山駅]]北側にも黒石山の黒石支群、エガミ田支群、モエサシ支群　等の多くの古墳が有り、[[新山古墳]]もある。 また、[[明治時代]]に黒石山の古墳の盗掘嫌疑で村人の事情聴取が行われた（結果無罪）が、小さな古墳の被葬者の名でさえ、その時は忘れられていたが、元々言い伝えがあったことが判る。築山古墳に関係する家伝等の調査が望まれる。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist}} == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)}} {{Japanese-history-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:つきやまこふん}} [[Category:馬見古墳群]] [[Category:奈良県の古墳]] [[Category:大和高田市の歴史]] [[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:陵墓参考地]]

'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, {{ill|Kashiba|ja|香芝市|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|Yamatotakada|ja|大和高田市|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

It is a [[zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.

== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/haniwa|qq=}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashihaka Kofun|qq=}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

revision.text:
'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==
* [[Chausuyama Kofun]] * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}}. ... 3 KB (402 words) - 20:39, 9 January 2025 * [[Umami Kofun Cluster|Umami Kofun Group]] ..

陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

[[Category:大和高田市の歴史]] [[Category:Kofun of Nara prefecture]] [[Category:Japanese imperial mausolea (unspecified)]] [[Category:馬見古墳群]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)}} [[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]]

'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Umami Kofun Group
revision.text:
'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. {{afc comment|Wikidata entry (none) Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Add wikidata]] Wikidata name: [[Category:Add wikidata]] [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==
* [[Chausuyama Kofun]] * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}}. ... 3 KB (402 words) - 20:39, 9 January 2025 * [[Umami Kofun Cluster|Umami Kofun Group]] ..
.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==
* [[Chausuyama Kofun]] * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}}. ... 3 KB (402 words) - 20:39, 9 January 2025 * [[Umami Kofun Cluster|Umami Kofun Group]] ..
.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January
== Notes ==
{{reflist}}
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==
* [[Chausuyama Kofun]] * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}}. ... 3 KB (402 words) - 20:39, 9 January 2025 * [[Umami Kofun Cluster|Umami Kofun Group]] ..
.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, [[:en:Kashiba, Nara|Kashiba]], and [[:en:Yamatotakada, Nara|Yamatotakada]] in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11603500]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 05:31, 12 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:07, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a [[:en:Zenpokoenfun|Zenpokoenfun]] (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the [[:en:Imperial Household Agency|Imperial Household Agency]] and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered [[:en:Haniwa|haniwa]] fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like [[:en:Hashihaka Kofun|Hashihaka Kofun]] and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun.
