== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]]
'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun}} (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency}} and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, {{ill|Kashiba, Nara|en|Kashiba, Nara|WD=Q696643|lt=Kashiba}}, and {{ill|Yamatotakada, Nara|en|Yamatotakada, Nara|WD=Q838669|lt=Yamatotakada}} in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Tsukiyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)]][[ja:築山古墳 (大和高田市)]]
'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|Zenpokoenfun|en|Zenpokoenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpokoenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.

The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January

'''Tsukiyama Kofun''' (築山古墳) is an ancient Kofun ([[kofun]]) located in Tsukiyama, Yamatotakada City, Nara Prefecture. It is a {{ill|Zenpōkōenfun|en|Zenpōkōenfun|WD=Q11268718|lt=Zenpōkōenfun|1=Zenpōkōenfun}} (keyhole-shaped kofun) and part of the {{ill|Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Cluster|lt=Umami Kofun Group|ja|馬見古墳群|1=Umami Kofun Cluster|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Group|13=User:Immanuelle/Umami Kofun Cluster|qq=}}, serving as the main tumulus of the southern cluster.
While the actual identity of the interred remains unknown, the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} designates the kofun as the "Iwazono no Misasagi Reference Site" (磐園陵墓参考地), associating it with Emperor Kenzo (the 23rd emperor of Japan).
== Overview == [[File:Tukiyama kofun 01.jpg|350px|thumb|right|Tsukiyama Kofun and surrounding kofun. {{National Land Aerial Photo}}]] The kofun is managed by the {{ill|Imperial Household Agency|en|Imperial Household Agency|WD=Q1143602|lt=Imperial Household Agency|1=Imperial Household Agency}} and closed to public access. The mound measures 210 meters in length, with a rear circle diameter of 120 meters and a front square width of 105 meters. Based on recovered {{ill|Haniwa|en|Haniwa|WD=Q849376|lt=haniwa|1=Haniwa}} fragments, it is estimated to have been constructed in the late 4th century.
The structure of the mound has not been fully investigated, but theories suggest combinations such as a two-tiered front and three-tiered rear construction or three tiers for both sections. Another theory proposes a three-tiered front and a four-tiered rear.
Historically, it is unclear, but evidence suggests the site may have been used as a fort, given its proximity to Nakagura Kita Pond and Babaseki, locations tied to local warriors. There is evidence of pathways and alterations that may have compromised the original mound shape. The surrounding moats, especially to the north, have been narrowed and altered.
The kofun is mentioned alongside {{ill|Kitsuneizuka Kofun|ja|狐井塚古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kitsuneizuka Kofun|qq=}}, a 75-meter zenpokoenfun to the south, in historical records and maps. These were collectively referred to as the northern and southern Kofuns of Kataoka. Names like Iwazono (磐園) and Iwatsukibashi (磐築橋) trace their origins to these Kofuns. During the Edo period, scattered haniwa and a large hole at the site indicate it had been looted.
== Cultural Significance == Tsukiyama Kofun aligns with notable geographical and cultural landmarks. The rear axis aligns with {{ill|Nijo Mountain|ja|二上山|1=Futakami Mountain|2=ja|3=二上山|lt=Mount Futakami (or Nijo)|comment=Kanji are ambiguous between the two pronounciations|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nijo Mountain|13=User:Immanuelle/Futakami Mountain|qq=}} near {{ill|Takenouchi Kaidō|ja|竹之内街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takenouchi Kaidō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竹内街道}}, and the front aligns with locations like {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|en|Hashihaka Kofun|WD=Q11603420|lt=Hashihaka Kofun|1=Hashihaka Kofun}} and {{ill|Hibara Shrine|ja|檜原神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hibara Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大神神社|1=Ōmiwa Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q705542}}. Nearby is the {{ill|Konpira Yama Kofun|ja|コンピラ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Yama Kofun|qq=}}, the largest round tumulus in the region, associated with {{ill|Kotohira shrines|lt=Kotohira shrines|lt=Konpira Shrine|ja|金毘羅神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konpira Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 金刀比羅神社}} and deities linked to navigation.
The kofun is surrounded by other tumuli, such as the {{ill|Inkiyama Kofun|ja|インキ山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inkiyama Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja|茶臼山古墳 (大和高田市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chausuyama Kofun (Yamatotakada)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and Kitsuneizuka Kofun. These structures hint at its prominence in the region's burial and cultural practices.
==See Also==

.ll|Kōryō, Nara|ja|広
陵町}}, {{ill|Kashiba, Nara|en|Kashiba, Nara|WD=Q696643|lt=Kashiba|1=Kashiba, Nara}}, and {{ill|Yamatotakada, Nara|en|Yamatotakada, Nara|WD=Q838669|lt=Yamatotakada|1=Yamatotakada, Nara}} in Nara Prefecture. The cluster is situated along the Umami Hills and its * {{ill|Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|ja|築山古墳|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukiyama Kofun (disambiguation)|qq=}} (Yamatotakada, Middle Kofun Period, keyhole-shaped, total length 210 m). ... 6 KB (745 words) - 21:16, 15 January
